title: 1. Visão Geral do Nível B2
overview:
O nível B2 do Quadro Europeu Comum de Referência para Línguas (CEFR) é classificado como **Intermediário Superior** (Upper-Intermediate). O aluno que atinge este nível deixa de ser um usuário básico e passa a ser um **usuário independente** da língua inglesa, capaz de operar com autonomia em ambientes acadêmicos, profissionais e sociais onde o inglês é a língua de comunicação.

### O que o aluno B2 é capaz de fazer

**Compreensão oral (Listening):**
- Compreender palestras, apresentações e discussões longas sobre temas complexos, desde que o assunto seja razoavelmente familiar.
- Acompanhar filmes, séries, documentários e noticiários em inglês sem legendas, entendendo a maior parte do conteúdo.
- Captar nuances de opinião, ironia e tom em conversas entre falantes nativos, mesmo quando falam em velocidade natural.
- Seguir argumentos complexos em debates e mesas-redondas, identificando os pontos principais e os argumentos de suporte.

**Compreensão escrita (Reading):**
- Ler artigos de jornal, revistas e sites sobre temas contemporâneos, compreendendo não apenas o sentido literal, mas também as atitudes e opiniões implícitas do autor.
- Entender textos técnicos e acadêmicos dentro de sua área de interesse ou especialização.
- Ler obras literárias contemporâneas em prosa, compreendendo enredo, personagens e temas.
- Interpretar gráficos, relatórios e documentos profissionais em inglês.

**Produção oral (Speaking):**
- Participar de conversas e discussões com falantes nativos com um grau de fluência e espontaneidade que torna a interação natural e confortável para ambas as partes.
- Apresentar descrições claras e detalhadas sobre uma ampla gama de assuntos relacionados aos seus interesses.
- Explicar um ponto de vista sobre um tema da atualidade, apresentando vantagens e desvantagens de diversas opções.
- Conduzir apresentações orais estruturadas, respondendo a perguntas da audiência com desenvoltura.
- Negociar, persuadir e argumentar em contextos profissionais.

**Produção escrita (Writing):**
- Escrever textos claros e detalhados sobre uma ampla variedade de assuntos relacionados aos seus interesses.
- Redigir ensaios, relatórios e resenhas, sintetizando informações de diversas fontes.
- Escrever e-mails formais e informais com tom adequado ao destinatário.
- Produzir textos argumentativos bem estruturados, com introdução, desenvolvimento e conclusão.

**Interação social e pragmática:**
- Usar a língua com flexibilidade e eficácia para fins sociais, incluindo uso emocional, alusivo e humorístico.
- Adaptar o registro linguístico (formal, semiformal, informal) de acordo com o contexto e o interlocutor.
- Lidar com mal-entendidos e situações imprevistas de comunicação, reformulando ou esclarecendo o que quis dizer.

### Diferença entre B1 e B2

Enquanto o aluno B1 consegue "sobreviver" em situações comunicativas, o aluno B2 consegue **prosperar** nelas. A diferença fundamental está na **profundidade** e na **autonomia**: o B2 não apenas entende e se faz entender, mas o faz com precisão, nuance e naturalidade. O aluno B2 comete menos erros gramaticais sistemáticos, possui um vocabulário significativamente mais amplo, e consegue lidar com abstrações e argumentações complexas.

---

grammar.title: 2.1 Mixed Conditionals (Condicionais Mistas)
grammar.content:
No nível B1, o aluno aprendeu as quatro condicionais básicas (Zero, First, Second e Third Conditional). No B2, é fundamental dominar as **condicionais mistas**, que combinam elementos de diferentes tipos de condicionais para expressar situações onde a condição e o resultado pertencem a tempos verbais distintos.

#### Tipo 1: Third Conditional + Second Conditional (Passado irreal → Presente irreal)

**Estrutura:** If + past perfect, would + base verb

Usa-se quando uma situação hipotética no passado tem um resultado hipotético no presente.

**Exemplos:**

1. If I had studied medicine, I would be a doctor now.
   *(Se eu tivesse estudado medicina, eu seria médico agora.)*

2. If she had accepted the job offer, she would be living in London.
   *(Se ela tivesse aceitado a oferta de emprego, estaria morando em Londres.)*

3. If we had invested in that company, we would be millionaires today.
   *(Se tivéssemos investido naquela empresa, seríamos milionários hoje.)*

4. If they had built the bridge, the commute would be much shorter.
   *(Se eles tivessem construído a ponte, o trajeto seria muito mais curto.)*

5. If I had learned to code as a teenager, I would have better career options now.
   *(Se eu tivesse aprendido a programar quando adolescente, teria melhores opções de carreira agora.)*

6. If he hadn't broken his leg, he would be playing in the tournament this weekend.
   *(Se ele não tivesse quebrado a perna, estaria jogando no torneio neste fim de semana.)*

7. If you had listened to my advice, you wouldn't be in this mess.
   *(Se você tivesse ouvido meu conselho, não estaria nessa confusão.)*

8. If the government had acted sooner, the crisis would be under control.
   *(Se o governo tivesse agido mais cedo, a crise estaria sob controle.)*

9. If I hadn't missed my flight, I would be on the beach right now.
   *(Se eu não tivesse perdido meu voo, estaria na praia agora.)*

10. If she had married him, she would be unhappy.
    *(Se ela tivesse casado com ele, estaria infeliz.)*

#### Tipo 2: Second Conditional + Third Conditional (Presente irreal → Passado irreal)

**Estrutura:** If + past simple, would have + past participle

Usa-se quando uma situação hipotética permanente ou geral no presente teria causado um resultado diferente no passado.

**Exemplos:**

1. If I were taller, I would have been accepted into the basketball team.
   *(Se eu fosse mais alto, teria sido aceito no time de basquete.)*

2. If she spoke French, she would have got the job in Paris.
   *(Se ela falasse francês, teria conseguido o emprego em Paris.)*

3. If he weren't so stubborn, he would have apologized by now.
   *(Se ele não fosse tão teimoso, já teria pedido desculpas.)*

4. If I didn't have a fear of flying, I would have visited Australia years ago.
   *(Se eu não tivesse medo de voar, teria visitado a Austrália anos atrás.)*

5. If we lived closer to the office, we wouldn't have been late this morning.
   *(Se morássemos mais perto do escritório, não teríamos chegado atrasados esta manhã.)*

6. If I were more organized, I would have finished the report on time.
   *(Se eu fosse mais organizado, teria terminado o relatório a tempo.)*

7. If she weren't so shy, she would have introduced herself to the CEO.
   *(Se ela não fosse tão tímida, teria se apresentado ao CEO.)*

8. If he were a better listener, he would have understood the instructions.
   *(Se ele soubesse ouvir melhor, teria entendido as instruções.)*

9. If they weren't so risk-averse, they would have invested in the startup.
   *(Se eles não fossem tão avessos ao risco, teriam investido na startup.)*

10. If I knew how to swim, I would have joined them at the lake.
    *(Se eu soubesse nadar, teria ido com eles ao lago.)*

---

grammar.title: 2.2 Wish / If only (Expressar Desejos e Arrependimentos)
grammar.content:
Estas estruturas expressam desejos sobre situações presentes, passadas ou futuras que o falante gostaria que fossem diferentes da realidade.

#### Wish / If only + Past Simple (desejo sobre o presente)

Expressa insatisfação com uma situação presente.

**Exemplos:**

1. I wish I had more free time.
   *(Quem dera eu tivesse mais tempo livre.)*

2. If only I spoke Japanese fluently.
   *(Se ao menos eu falasse japonês fluentemente.)*

3. She wishes she lived in a bigger city.
   *(Ela queria morar em uma cidade maior.)*

4. I wish I didn't have to work tomorrow.
   *(Quem dera eu não tivesse que trabalhar amanhã.)*

5. He wishes he were taller.
   *(Ele queria ser mais alto.)*

6. If only we had enough money to travel the world.
   *(Se ao menos tivéssemos dinheiro suficiente para viajar pelo mundo.)*

7. I wish I knew the answer to that question.
   *(Quem dera eu soubesse a resposta para aquela pergunta.)*

8. They wish they owned a house by the sea.
   *(Eles gostariam de ter uma casa à beira-mar.)*

9. If only my boss were more understanding.
   *(Se ao menos meu chefe fosse mais compreensivo.)*

10. She wishes she weren't so afraid of public speaking.
    *(Ela queria não ter tanto medo de falar em público.)*

**Nota:** Após "wish" e "if only", usamos **were** para todas as pessoas (I, he, she, it) em contextos formais, embora "was" seja aceito informalmente.

#### Wish / If only + Past Perfect (arrependimento sobre o passado)

Expressa arrependimento sobre algo que aconteceu (ou não aconteceu) no passado.

**Exemplos:**

1. I wish I had studied harder for the exam.
   *(Quem dera eu tivesse estudado mais para a prova.)*

2. If only she had told me the truth from the beginning.
   *(Se ao menos ela tivesse me contado a verdade desde o início.)*

3. He wishes he hadn't sold his old guitar.
   *(Ele queria não ter vendido sua guitarra antiga.)*

4. I wish I had taken that opportunity when I had the chance.
   *(Quem dera eu tivesse aproveitado aquela oportunidade quando tive a chance.)*

5. If only we had left the house earlier.
   *(Se ao menos tivéssemos saído de casa mais cedo.)*

6. She wishes she had learned to drive when she was younger.
   *(Ela queria ter aprendido a dirigir quando era mais nova.)*

7. I wish I hadn't said those things to her.
   *(Quem dera eu não tivesse dito aquelas coisas para ela.)*

8. He wishes he had spent more time with his grandparents.
   *(Ele queria ter passado mais tempo com seus avós.)*

9. If only they had listened to the weather forecast.
   *(Se ao menos eles tivessem ouvido a previsão do tempo.)*

10. We wish we had booked the hotel in advance.
    *(Gostaríamos de ter reservado o hotel com antecedência.)*

#### Wish / If only + Would (desejo de mudança de comportamento/situação futura)

Expressa irritação com uma situação presente ou desejo de que alguém (ou algo) mude de comportamento. Não se usa "wish + would" quando o sujeito de "wish" é o mesmo de "would".

**Exemplos:**

1. I wish you would stop making so much noise.
   *(Eu queria que você parasse de fazer tanto barulho.)*

2. If only it would stop raining.
   *(Se ao menos parasse de chover.)*

3. She wishes her neighbors would turn down the music.
   *(Ela queria que os vizinhos abaixassem a música.)*

4. I wish the bus would come on time for once.
   *(Quem dera o ônibus chegasse na hora pelo menos uma vez.)*

5. He wishes his colleagues would be more cooperative.
   *(Ele queria que seus colegas fossem mais cooperativos.)*

6. If only the government would invest more in education.
   *(Se ao menos o governo investisse mais em educação.)*

7. I wish you wouldn't interrupt me when I'm speaking.
   *(Eu queria que você não me interrompesse quando estou falando.)*

8. She wishes her son would tidy his room without being asked.
   *(Ela queria que o filho arrumasse o quarto sem precisar pedir.)*

9. I wish people would be more considerate of others.
   *(Quem dera as pessoas fossem mais atenciosas com os outros.)*

10. If only this headache would go away.
    *(Se ao menos essa dor de cabeça passasse.)*

---

grammar.title: 2.3 Reported Speech Avançado (Discurso Indireto Avançado)
grammar.content:
No nível B1, o aluno aprendeu as transformações básicas do discurso direto para o indireto. No B2, aprofundamos com verbos de relato mais sofisticados, padrões complexos e situações especiais.

#### Verbos de relato avançados e seus padrões

**Padrão 1: Verb + infinitive (to do)**
Verbos: agree, offer, promise, refuse, threaten, claim, demand

**Exemplos:**

1. "I'll help you move." → He **offered to help** me move.
   *(Ele se ofereceu para me ajudar com a mudança.)*

2. "I won't tell anyone." → She **promised not to tell** anyone.
   *(Ela prometeu não contar a ninguém.)*

3. "I won't do it." → He **refused to do** it.
   *(Ele se recusou a fazer isso.)*

4. "I'll call the police if you don't leave." → She **threatened to call** the police.
   *(Ela ameaçou chamar a polícia.)*

5. "I didn't steal the money." → He **claimed not to have stolen** the money.
   *(Ele alegou não ter roubado o dinheiro.)*

6. "Yes, I'll attend the meeting." → She **agreed to attend** the meeting.
   *(Ela concordou em participar da reunião.)*

7. "Give me my money back!" → He **demanded to get** his money back.
   *(Ele exigiu receber seu dinheiro de volta.)*

8. "I'll finish the report by Friday." → She **promised to finish** the report by Friday.
   *(Ela prometeu terminar o relatório até sexta.)*

9. "I won't sign the contract." → He **refused to sign** the contract.
   *(Ele se recusou a assinar o contrato.)*

10. "I'll fire you if you're late again." → The boss **threatened to fire** him.
    *(O chefe ameaçou demiti-lo.)*

**Padrão 2: Verb + object + infinitive (to do)**
Verbos: advise, encourage, invite, remind, warn, persuade, convince, urge, order, forbid, allow

**Exemplos:**

1. "You should see a doctor." → She **advised me to see** a doctor.
   *(Ela me aconselhou a ir ao médico.)*

2. "Don't forget to lock the door." → He **reminded me to lock** the door.
   *(Ele me lembrou de trancar a porta.)*

3. "Be careful with the ice on the road." → She **warned us to be** careful with the ice.
   *(Ela nos alertou para termos cuidado com o gelo na estrada.)*

4. "Would you like to come to my party?" → He **invited her to come** to his party.
   *(Ele a convidou para ir à festa dele.)*

5. "You really should apply for the scholarship." → My teacher **encouraged me to apply** for the scholarship.
   *(Minha professora me encorajou a me candidatar à bolsa.)*

6. "Come on, let's go! It'll be fun!" → She **persuaded me to go**.
   *(Ela me convenceu a ir.)*

7. "Leave the room immediately!" → The officer **ordered them to leave** the room.
   *(O oficial os mandou sair da sala.)*

8. "You are not allowed to use your phone here." → The teacher **forbade them to use** their phones.
   *(A professora os proibiu de usar seus celulares.)*

9. "You must act now before it's too late." → She **urged him to act** before it was too late.
   *(Ela o instou a agir antes que fosse tarde demais.)*

10. "You can leave early today." → The manager **allowed us to leave** early.
    *(O gerente nos permitiu sair mais cedo.)*

**Padrão 3: Verb + gerund (-ing)**
Verbos: admit, deny, suggest, recommend, regret, apologize for, insist on, accuse (someone) of

**Exemplos:**

1. "Yes, I broke the window." → He **admitted breaking** the window.
   *(Ele admitiu ter quebrado a janela.)*

2. "I didn't take your book." → She **denied taking** my book.
   *(Ela negou ter pegado meu livro.)*

3. "Why don't we go to the Italian restaurant?" → He **suggested going** to the Italian restaurant.
   *(Ele sugeriu ir ao restaurante italiano.)*

4. "You should try the seafood pasta." → She **recommended trying** the seafood pasta.
   *(Ela recomendou experimentar a massa com frutos do mar.)*

5. "I'm sorry I arrived late." → He **apologized for arriving** late.
   *(Ele pediu desculpas por ter chegado atrasado.)*

6. "I'm sorry I didn't call you sooner." → She **regretted not calling** me sooner.
   *(Ela lamentou não ter me ligado antes.)*

7. "You must pay for the damages." → They **insisted on my paying** for the damages.
   *(Eles insistiram que eu pagasse pelos danos.)*

8. "You stole the documents!" → She **accused him of stealing** the documents.
   *(Ela o acusou de roubar os documentos.)*

9. "Let's take a break." → He **suggested taking** a break.
   *(Ele sugeriu fazer uma pausa.)*

10. "I'm sorry for the misunderstanding." → She **apologized for the misunderstanding**.
    *(Ela pediu desculpas pelo mal-entendido.)*

**Padrão 4: Verb + (that) + clause**
Verbos: explain, mention, complain, boast, point out, insist, suggest, recommend

Quando "suggest" e "recommend" são usados neste padrão, o verbo na cláusula que segue fica no subjuntivo (forma base) ou com "should":

**Exemplos:**

1. "The meeting has been postponed." → He **explained that** the meeting had been postponed.
   *(Ele explicou que a reunião tinha sido adiada.)*

2. "By the way, I saw your ex at the party." → She **mentioned that** she had seen my ex at the party.
   *(Ela mencionou que tinha visto meu/minha ex na festa.)*

3. "The service here is terrible!" → He **complained that** the service was terrible.
   *(Ele reclamou que o serviço era terrível.)*

4. "I've been promoted three times this year." → She **boasted that** she had been promoted three times that year.
   *(Ela se gabou de ter sido promovida três vezes naquele ano.)*

5. "You haven't submitted your assignment." → The teacher **pointed out that** I hadn't submitted my assignment.
   *(A professora apontou que eu não tinha entregado meu trabalho.)*

6. "You should see a specialist." → The doctor **recommended that** I **(should) see** a specialist.
   *(O médico recomendou que eu consultasse um especialista.)*

7. "We should leave early." → He **suggested that** we **(should) leave** early.
   *(Ele sugeriu que saíssemos cedo.)*

8. "I didn't do it, and that's final." → He **insisted that** he hadn't done it.
   *(Ele insistiu que não tinha feito aquilo.)*

9. "There's a problem with the data." → She **pointed out that** there was a problem with the data.
   *(Ela apontou que havia um problema com os dados.)*

10. "I can run faster than anyone on the team." → He **boasted that** he could run faster than anyone on the team.
    *(Ele se gabou de que conseguia correr mais rápido que qualquer um do time.)*

---

grammar.title: 2.4 Passive Voice — Estruturas Avançadas
grammar.content:
No B2, o aluno deve dominar não apenas a voz passiva simples, mas também estruturas passivas complexas e impessoais.

#### Passive with reporting verbs (It is said that... / He is said to...)

Estas estruturas são muito comuns em textos jornalísticos e acadêmicos. Usam-se para relatar informações de fonte não especificada.

**Estrutura 1:** It + is/was + past participle + that + clause
**Estrutura 2:** Subject + is/was + past participle + to + infinitive

**Exemplos:**

1. People say he is very wealthy. →
   **It is said that** he is very wealthy. / **He is said to be** very wealthy.
   *(Dizem que ele é muito rico.)*

2. People believe the company is planning layoffs. →
   **It is believed that** the company is planning layoffs. / **The company is believed to be planning** layoffs.
   *(Acredita-se que a empresa está planejando demissões.)*

3. They reported that the suspect had fled the country. →
   **It was reported that** the suspect had fled the country. / **The suspect was reported to have fled** the country.
   *(Foi relatado que o suspeito tinha fugido do país.)*

4. People think she is the best candidate. →
   **It is thought that** she is the best candidate. / **She is thought to be** the best candidate.
   *(Acredita-se que ela é a melhor candidata.)*

5. They expected him to resign. →
   **It was expected that** he would resign. / **He was expected to resign.**
   *(Esperava-se que ele renunciasse.)*

6. People know that exercise improves mental health. →
   **It is known that** exercise improves mental health. / **Exercise is known to improve** mental health.
   *(Sabe-se que exercício melhora a saúde mental.)*

7. They allege that the politician accepted bribes. →
   **It is alleged that** the politician accepted bribes. / **The politician is alleged to have accepted** bribes.
   *(Alega-se que o político aceitou subornos.)*

8. People consider him to be a genius. →
   **It is considered that** he is a genius. / **He is considered to be** a genius.
   *(Ele é considerado um gênio.)*

9. They estimated that the project would cost two million dollars. →
   **It was estimated that** the project would cost two million. / **The project was estimated to cost** two million.
   *(Estimou-se que o projeto custaria dois milhões de dólares.)*

10. People suppose she left the country. →
    **It is supposed that** she left the country. / **She is supposed to have left** the country.
    *(Supõe-se que ela tenha deixado o país.)*

#### Have / Get something done (Causative Passive)

Usa-se para indicar que alguém organizou ou contratou outra pessoa para fazer algo por ela.

**Estrutura:** Subject + have/get + object + past participle

**Exemplos:**

1. I **had my car repaired** last week.
   *(Mandei consertar meu carro na semana passada.)*

2. She **got her hair cut** at a new salon.
   *(Ela cortou o cabelo em um salão novo.)* [= mandou cortar]

3. We're going to **have the house painted** next month.
   *(Vamos mandar pintar a casa no mês que vem.)*

4. He **had his suit dry-cleaned** for the wedding.
   *(Ele mandou limpar o terno a seco para o casamento.)*

5. You should **get your eyes tested** regularly.
   *(Você deveria mandar examinar seus olhos regularmente.)*

6. They **had their kitchen remodeled** by a professional.
   *(Eles mandaram reformar a cozinha por um profissional.)*

7. I need to **have my passport renewed** before the trip.
   *(Preciso mandar renovar meu passaporte antes da viagem.)*

8. She **got her nails done** for the party.
   *(Ela fez as unhas para a festa.)* [= mandou fazer]

9. We **had the documents translated** into English.
   *(Mandamos traduzir os documentos para o inglês.)*

10. He **got his phone screen replaced** at the electronics store.
    *(Ele mandou trocar a tela do celular na loja de eletrônicos.)*

**Nota:** "Have something done" também pode expressar uma experiência negativa sofrida involuntariamente:
- I **had my wallet stolen** on the subway. *(Tive minha carteira roubada no metrô.)*
- She **had her car broken into** last night. *(O carro dela foi arrombado ontem à noite.)*

---

grammar.title: 2.5 Relative Clauses — Uso Avançado
grammar.content:
#### Defining vs. Non-defining Relative Clauses (Revisão aprofundada)

**Defining (restritivas):** essenciais para identificar o substantivo. Sem vírgulas.
**Non-defining (explicativas):** adicionam informação extra. Com vírgulas. Nunca usam "that".

**Exemplos de Non-defining (foco do B2):**

1. My brother, **who lives in Canada**, is coming to visit us.
   *(Meu irmão, que mora no Canadá, está vindo nos visitar.)*

2. The Eiffel Tower, **which was built in 1889**, attracts millions of tourists every year.
   *(A Torre Eiffel, que foi construída em 1889, atrai milhões de turistas todo ano.)*

3. Professor Williams, **whose research focuses on climate change**, gave an inspiring lecture.
   *(O Professor Williams, cuja pesquisa é focada em mudanças climáticas, deu uma palestra inspiradora.)*

4. The company offered me a promotion, **which I gladly accepted**.
   *(A empresa me ofereceu uma promoção, a qual eu aceitei com prazer.)*

5. We stayed at the Grand Hotel, **where my parents spent their honeymoon**.
   *(Ficamos no Grand Hotel, onde meus pais passaram a lua de mel.)*

6. Last July, **when the heatwave hit**, many people were hospitalized.
   *(Em julho passado, quando a onda de calor atingiu, muitas pessoas foram hospitalizadas.)*

7. Her latest novel, **which has already sold a million copies**, is being adapted into a film.
   *(Seu último romance, que já vendeu um milhão de cópias, está sendo adaptado para um filme.)*

8. The CEO, **who is known for her philanthropic work**, announced a major donation.
   *(A CEO, que é conhecida por seu trabalho filantrópico, anunciou uma grande doação.)*

9. The painting, **which had been missing for decades**, was found in an attic in Florence.
   *(A pintura, que estava desaparecida há décadas, foi encontrada em um sótão em Florença.)*

10. My neighbor's dog, **which is a golden retriever**, is the friendliest animal I've ever met.
    *(O cachorro do meu vizinho, que é um golden retriever, é o animal mais amigável que já conheci.)*

#### Reduced Relative Clauses (Orações relativas reduzidas)

Em inglês formal e escrito, as orações relativas podem ser reduzidas, removendo o pronome relativo e o verbo auxiliar.

**Active:** The man **who is standing** by the door → The man **standing** by the door
**Passive:** The book **which was written** by Orwell → The book **written** by Orwell

**Exemplos:**

1. The woman **sitting** next to me on the plane was a famous actress.
   *(A mulher sentada ao meu lado no avião era uma atriz famosa.)*

2. The letter **sent** to the wrong address was returned a week later.
   *(A carta enviada ao endereço errado foi devolvida uma semana depois.)*

3. Students **wishing** to apply must submit their forms by Friday.
   *(Alunos que desejam se candidatar devem entregar seus formulários até sexta.)*

4. The car **parked** in front of the building belongs to my boss.
   *(O carro estacionado em frente ao prédio é do meu chefe.)*

5. Anyone **caught** cheating will be immediately disqualified.
   *(Qualquer pessoa flagrada colando será imediatamente desqualificada.)*

6. The products **manufactured** in this factory meet the highest quality standards.
   *(Os produtos fabricados nesta fábrica atendem aos mais altos padrões de qualidade.)*

7. The tourists **visiting** the museum were impressed by the collection.
   *(Os turistas visitando o museu ficaram impressionados com a coleção.)*

8. The report **published** last week contains some alarming data.
   *(O relatório publicado na semana passada contém dados alarmantes.)*

9. People **living** in rural areas often have limited access to healthcare.
   *(Pessoas que moram em áreas rurais frequentemente têm acesso limitado a serviços de saúde.)*

10. The money **raised** at the charity event will be donated to the hospital.
    *(O dinheiro arrecadado no evento beneficente será doado ao hospital.)*

#### Prepositions in Relative Clauses

No inglês formal, a preposição pode vir antes do pronome relativo. No inglês informal, fica no final.

**Exemplos:**

1. **Formal:** The company **for which** he works is based in New York.
   **Informal:** The company **which** he works **for** is based in New York.
   *(A empresa para a qual ele trabalha fica em Nova York.)*

2. **Formal:** The topic **about which** they were arguing was controversial.
   **Informal:** The topic **which** they were arguing **about** was controversial.
   *(O assunto sobre o qual eles estavam discutindo era controverso.)*

3. **Formal:** The person **to whom** I spoke was very helpful.
   **Informal:** The person **who** I spoke **to** was very helpful.
   *(A pessoa com quem falei foi muito prestativa.)*

4. **Formal:** The chair **on which** she was sitting suddenly broke.
   **Informal:** The chair **which** she was sitting **on** suddenly broke.
   *(A cadeira na qual ela estava sentada quebrou de repente.)*

5. **Formal:** The project **in which** she is involved has received international funding.
   **Informal:** The project **which** she is involved **in** has received international funding.
   *(O projeto no qual ela está envolvida recebeu financiamento internacional.)*

6. The woman **with whom** he traveled across Europe became his wife.
   *(A mulher com quem ele viajou pela Europa se tornou sua esposa.)*

7. The reason **for which** the meeting was canceled has not been disclosed.
   *(O motivo pelo qual a reunião foi cancelada não foi revelado.)*

8. The school **at which** she studied is one of the oldest in the country.
   *(A escola na qual ela estudou é uma das mais antigas do país.)*

9. The issues **about which** we complained have finally been addressed.
   *(As questões sobre as quais reclamamos finalmente foram resolvidas.)*

10. The artist **by whom** this painting was created remains unknown.
    *(O artista por quem esta pintura foi criada permanece desconhecido.)*

---

grammar.title: 2.6 Modals of Deduction and Speculation (Modais de Dedução e Especulação)
grammar.content:
No B2, os modais são usados de forma mais sofisticada para expressar graus de certeza, probabilidade e especulação sobre o presente e o passado.

#### Present Deduction

| Modal | Grau de certeza | Exemplo |
|-------|----------------|---------|
| must | quase certo (afirmativo) | She must be at home. |
| can't / couldn't | quase certo (negativo) | He can't be serious. |
| may / might / could | possível | They might be lost. |

**Exemplos:**

1. She's not answering her phone. She **must be** in a meeting.
   *(Ela não está atendendo o telefone. Ela deve estar em uma reunião.)*

2. He **can't be** the thief. He was with me all evening.
   *(Ele não pode ser o ladrão. Ele estava comigo a noite toda.)*

3. They're very late. They **might be** stuck in traffic.
   *(Eles estão muito atrasados. Podem estar presos no trânsito.)*

4. She speaks with a British accent. She **could be** from England.
   *(Ela fala com sotaque britânico. Ela pode ser da Inglaterra.)*

5. You've been working all day. You **must be** exhausted.
   *(Você trabalhou o dia todo. Você deve estar exausto.)*

6. He's wearing a uniform. He **must be** a security guard.
   *(Ele está usando uniforme. Ele deve ser um segurança.)*

7. She **can't be** over 30. She looks so young!
   *(Ela não pode ter mais de 30. Ela parece tão jovem!)*

8. That restaurant **may be** closed on Mondays. Let's check online.
   *(Aquele restaurante pode estar fechado às segundas. Vamos verificar online.)*

9. They **could be** planning a surprise for you.
   *(Eles podem estar planejando uma surpresa para você.)*

10. There's no way that's true. He **couldn't be** lying to us.
    *(Não é possível que isso seja verdade. Ele não pode estar mentindo para nós.)*

#### Past Deduction

| Modal | Grau de certeza | Exemplo |
|-------|----------------|---------|
| must have + pp | quase certo (afirmativo) | She must have left. |
| can't have / couldn't have + pp | quase certo (negativo) | He can't have known. |
| may have / might have / could have + pp | possível | They might have forgotten. |

**Exemplos:**

1. She's not home. She **must have gone** out.
   *(Ela não está em casa. Ela deve ter saído.)*

2. He **can't have passed** the test. He didn't study at all.
   *(Ele não pode ter passado na prova. Ele não estudou nada.)*

3. They **might have missed** the train.
   *(Eles podem ter perdido o trem.)*

4. Someone **must have taken** my umbrella by mistake.
   *(Alguém deve ter pegado meu guarda-chuva por engano.)*

5. She **couldn't have known** about the party. It was a secret.
   *(Ela não pode ter sabido da festa. Era segredo.)*

6. He didn't come to work. He **may have been** feeling unwell.
   *(Ele não veio trabalhar. Pode ser que estivesse se sentindo mal.)*

7. The streets are wet. It **must have rained** during the night.
   *(As ruas estão molhadas. Deve ter chovido durante a noite.)*

8. She **could have left** her phone at the restaurant.
   *(Ela pode ter deixado o celular no restaurante.)*

9. They **can't have finished** the project already. It's too soon.
   *(Eles não podem ter terminado o projeto já. É cedo demais.)*

10. He **might have been** joking when he said that.
    *(Ele pode ter estado brincando quando disse aquilo.)*

---

grammar.title: 2.7 Future Forms — Nuances Avançadas
grammar.content:
No B2, o aluno deve distinguir com precisão entre as diversas formas de expressar futuro, dominando nuances que vão além do básico.

#### Future Continuous (will be + -ing)

Descreve uma ação que estará em andamento em um momento específico no futuro. Também é usado para perguntar educadamente sobre os planos de alguém.

**Exemplos:**

1. This time tomorrow, I **will be flying** to Barcelona.
   *(Amanhã a esta hora, estarei voando para Barcelona.)*

2. Don't call me at 8. I **will be having** dinner.
   *(Não me ligue às 8. Estarei jantando.)*

3. They **will be presenting** the new product at the conference next week.
   *(Eles estarão apresentando o novo produto na conferência semana que vem.)*

4. **Will you be using** the car this afternoon? (pergunta educada)
   *(Você vai estar usando o carro esta tarde?)*

5. By this time next year, she **will be working** at a hospital.
   *(A esta altura do ano que vem, ela estará trabalhando em um hospital.)*

6. I **won't be attending** the meeting because I have a doctor's appointment.
   *(Não estarei presente na reunião porque tenho uma consulta médica.)*

7. At midnight, we **will be celebrating** the New Year.
   *(À meia-noite, estaremos comemorando o Ano Novo.)*

8. **Will you be passing** by the supermarket on your way home?
   *(Você vai passar pelo supermercado no caminho para casa?)*

9. The workers **will be installing** the new system all week.
   *(Os trabalhadores estarão instalando o novo sistema a semana toda.)*

10. This time next month, I **will be lying** on a beach in Thailand.
    *(Nesta mesma hora no mês que vem, estarei deitado em uma praia na Tailândia.)*

#### Future Perfect (will have + past participle)

Descreve uma ação que estará concluída antes de um ponto específico no futuro.

**Exemplos:**

1. By the end of this year, I **will have saved** enough money to buy a car.
   *(Até o final deste ano, terei economizado dinheiro suficiente para comprar um carro.)*

2. She **will have graduated** by the time the project starts.
   *(Ela já terá se formado quando o projeto começar.)*

3. By 2030, scientists **will have developed** more efficient renewable energy sources.
   *(Até 2030, cientistas terão desenvolvido fontes de energia renovável mais eficientes.)*

4. We **will have been** married for 25 years next June.
   *(Em junho que vem, estaremos casados há 25 anos.)*

5. He **will have finished** the book by the time the movie comes out.
   *(Ele terá terminado o livro quando o filme for lançado.)*

6. By tomorrow morning, the storm **will have passed**.
   *(Até amanhã de manhã, a tempestade terá passado.)*

7. They **will have completed** the construction by December.
   *(Eles terão concluído a construção até dezembro.)*

8. I **will have read** all the required books before the course begins.
   *(Terei lido todos os livros exigidos antes do curso começar.)*

9. By the time you arrive, we **will have already eaten**.
   *(Quando você chegar, já teremos comido.)*

10. She **will have worked** here for ten years by next April.
    *(Ela terá trabalhado aqui por dez anos em abril que vem.)*

#### Future Perfect Continuous (will have been + -ing)

Enfatiza a **duração** de uma ação que estará em andamento até um ponto no futuro.

**Exemplos:**

1. By June, I **will have been living** in this city for five years.
   *(Em junho, estarei morando nesta cidade há cinco anos.)*

2. She **will have been studying** English for ten years by the time she graduates.
   *(Ela estará estudando inglês há dez anos quando se formar.)*

3. By the end of this shift, he **will have been working** for 12 hours straight.
   *(Até o final deste turno, ele estará trabalhando por 12 horas seguidas.)*

4. Next month, they **will have been dating** for two years.
   *(No mês que vem, eles estarão namorando há dois anos.)*

5. By Friday, we **will have been waiting** for a response for three weeks.
   *(Até sexta, estaremos esperando uma resposta há três semanas.)*

6. She **will have been teaching** at this school for 20 years next September.
   *(Ela estará lecionando nesta escola há 20 anos em setembro que vem.)*

7. By the time the project is done, the team **will have been working** on it for over a year.
   *(Quando o projeto estiver pronto, a equipe estará trabalhando nele há mais de um ano.)*

8. He **will have been training** for the marathon for six months by race day.
   *(Ele estará treinando para a maratona há seis meses no dia da corrida.)*

9. By the time you read this, I **will have been traveling** for 24 hours.
   *(Quando você ler isto, eu estarei viajando há 24 horas.)*

10. Next year, the company **will have been operating** for half a century.
    *(No ano que vem, a empresa estará operando há meio século.)*

---

grammar.title: 2.8 Cleft Sentences (Orações Clivadas)
grammar.content:
Cleft sentences são usadas para dar ênfase a uma parte específica da frase. Muito comuns na escrita formal e na fala enfática.

#### It-cleft

**Estrutura:** It + is/was + elemento enfatizado + that/who + restante da frase

**Exemplos:**

1. **It was John who** broke the vase, not Peter.
   *(Foi o John quem quebrou o vaso, não o Peter.)*

2. **It is the lack of funding that** is causing the problem.
   *(É a falta de financiamento que está causando o problema.)*

3. **It was in 1969 that** humans first walked on the moon.
   *(Foi em 1969 que os seres humanos pisaram na Lua pela primeira vez.)*

4. **It was her dedication that** impressed the interviewers.
   *(Foi a dedicação dela que impressionou os entrevistadores.)*

5. **It is only when you lose something that** you realize its value.
   *(É só quando você perde algo que percebe seu valor.)*

6. **It wasn't the salary that** attracted me to the job; **it was** the opportunity to travel.
   *(Não foi o salário que me atraiu para o emprego; foi a oportunidade de viajar.)*

7. **It was my mother who** taught me to always be honest.
   *(Foi minha mãe quem me ensinou a ser sempre honesto.)*

8. **It is this report that** the board needs to review urgently.
   *(É este relatório que o conselho precisa revisar urgentemente.)*

9. **It was because of the traffic that** we missed the show.
   *(Foi por causa do trânsito que perdemos o show.)*

10. **It is education that** will change the future of this community.
    *(É a educação que vai mudar o futuro desta comunidade.)*

#### What-cleft (Pseudo-cleft)

**Estrutura:** What + sujeito + verbo + is/was + elemento enfatizado

**Exemplos:**

1. **What I need** is a good night's sleep.
   *(O que eu preciso é de uma boa noite de sono.)*

2. **What she said** was completely out of line.
   *(O que ela disse foi completamente inadequado.)*

3. **What annoys me most** is people who are always late.
   *(O que mais me irrita são pessoas que estão sempre atrasadas.)*

4. **What we should focus on** is improving customer satisfaction.
   *(O que devemos focar é em melhorar a satisfação do cliente.)*

5. **What happened next** was totally unexpected.
   *(O que aconteceu em seguida foi totalmente inesperado.)*

6. **What the country needs** is strong leadership.
   *(O que o país precisa é de liderança forte.)*

7. **What surprised me** was how calm she remained under pressure.
   *(O que me surpreendeu foi como ela permaneceu calma sob pressão.)*

8. **What you should do** is talk to a lawyer.
   *(O que você deveria fazer é conversar com um advogado.)*

9. **What matters most** is that everyone is safe.
   *(O que mais importa é que todos estejam seguros.)*

10. **What they didn't realize** was that the camera was recording.
    *(O que eles não perceberam foi que a câmera estava gravando.)*

---

grammar.title: 2.9 Inversion (Inversão de Sujeito e Verbo)
grammar.content:
A inversão é usada em inglês formal e literário para dar ênfase. O verbo auxiliar vem antes do sujeito, como em perguntas, mas a frase é declarativa.

#### Inversão com advérbios negativos ou restritivos

Quando advérbios como **never, rarely, seldom, hardly, barely, scarcely, no sooner, not only, under no circumstances, at no time, in no way, on no account, little** iniciam a frase, ocorre inversão.

**Exemplos:**

1. **Never have I seen** such a beautiful sunset.
   *(Nunca vi um pôr do sol tão lindo.)*

2. **Rarely does she** complain about anything.
   *(Raramente ela reclama de qualquer coisa.)*

3. **Seldom do** we get the chance to relax.
   *(Poucas vezes temos a chance de relaxar.)*

4. **Hardly had** I sat down **when** the phone rang.
   *(Mal eu tinha sentado quando o telefone tocou.)*

5. **No sooner had** he arrived **than** it started raining.
   *(Mal ele tinha chegado e começou a chover.)*

6. **Not only did** she win the competition, **but she also** broke the record.
   *(Ela não apenas venceu a competição, como também quebrou o recorde.)*

7. **Under no circumstances should** you open this door.
   *(Sob nenhuma circunstância você deve abrir esta porta.)*

8. **At no time did** the defendant show any remorse.
   *(Em nenhum momento o réu demonstrou qualquer remorso.)*

9. **In no way am** I responsible for this mistake.
   *(De forma alguma sou responsável por este erro.)*

10. **Little did** he know that his life was about to change forever.
    *(Ele mal sabia que sua vida estava prestes a mudar para sempre.)*

#### Inversão com "only"

**Exemplos:**

1. **Only after** the meeting **did** I realize the significance of the decision.
   *(Só depois da reunião percebi a importância da decisão.)*

2. **Only when** she left **did** I understand how much she meant to me.
   *(Só quando ela foi embora entendi o quanto ela significava para mim.)*

3. **Only by** working together **can** we solve this problem.
   *(Só trabalhando juntos podemos resolver este problema.)*

4. **Only then did** he appreciate what he had lost.
   *(Só então ele reconheceu o que tinha perdido.)*

5. **Only if** you study hard **will** you pass the exam.
   *(Só se você estudar muito conseguirá passar na prova.)*

6. **Only in** this restaurant **can** you find such authentic cuisine.
   *(Só neste restaurante você encontra uma culinária tão autêntica.)*

7. **Only recently have** scientists discovered the cause of the disease.
   *(Só recentemente os cientistas descobriram a causa da doença.)*

8. **Only with** great effort **did** they manage to finish on time.
   *(Só com grande esforço eles conseguiram terminar a tempo.)*

9. **Only once did** she mention her childhood.
   *(Apenas uma vez ela mencionou sua infância.)*

10. **Only later did** we find out the truth.
    *(Só depois descobrimos a verdade.)*

---

grammar.title: 2.10 Participle Clauses (Orações Participiais)
grammar.content:
Participle clauses são usadas para tornar a escrita mais concisa e elegante. São muito comuns em textos formais e literários.

#### Present participle (-ing) — ação simultânea ou causa

**Exemplos:**

1. **Feeling tired**, she decided to go to bed early.
   *(Sentindo-se cansada, ela decidiu ir para a cama cedo.)*

2. **Walking through the park**, I noticed a beautiful bird.
   *(Caminhando pelo parque, notei um pássaro lindo.)*

3. **Not knowing what to say**, he remained silent.
   *(Não sabendo o que dizer, ele permaneceu em silêncio.)*

4. **Having lived** in Japan for five years, she speaks Japanese fluently.
   *(Tendo morado no Japão por cinco anos, ela fala japonês fluentemente.)*

5. **Being a vegetarian**, she doesn't eat meat.
   *(Sendo vegetariana, ela não come carne.)*

6. **Realizing** his mistake, the student corrected his answer.
   *(Percebendo seu erro, o aluno corrigiu sua resposta.)*

7. **Driving** to work, I heard the news on the radio.
   *(Dirigindo para o trabalho, ouvi a notícia no rádio.)*

8. **Seeing** that nobody was around, the cat jumped on the table.
   *(Vendo que não havia ninguém por perto, o gato pulou na mesa.)*

9. **Working overtime** every day, she eventually burned out.
   *(Fazendo hora extra todo dia, ela eventualmente ficou esgotada.)*

10. **Having completed** the training, the employees were ready for their new roles.
    *(Tendo completado o treinamento, os funcionários estavam prontos para suas novas funções.)*

#### Past participle (-ed / irregular) — ação passiva ou resultado

**Exemplos:**

1. **Built in the 15th century**, the castle is one of the oldest in the region.
   *(Construído no século XV, o castelo é um dos mais antigos da região.)*

2. **Exhausted by the long journey**, the travelers fell asleep immediately.
   *(Exaustos pela longa viagem, os viajantes adormeceram imediatamente.)*

3. **Shocked by the news**, she couldn't say a word.
   *(Chocada com a notícia, ela não conseguiu dizer uma palavra.)*

4. **Designed by a famous architect**, the building won several awards.
   *(Projetado por um arquiteto famoso, o edifício ganhou vários prêmios.)*

5. **Encouraged by her success**, she decided to start her own business.
   *(Encorajada pelo seu sucesso, ela decidiu abrir seu próprio negócio.)*

6. **Written in plain English**, the manual is easy to understand.
   *(Escrito em inglês simples, o manual é fácil de entender.)*

7. **Abandoned by its owners**, the dog was found wandering the streets.
   *(Abandonado por seus donos, o cachorro foi encontrado vagando pelas ruas.)*

8. **Impressed by her qualifications**, the company offered her the position.
   *(Impressionados com suas qualificações, a empresa lhe ofereceu a posição.)*

9. **Situated on the coast**, the town offers spectacular views of the ocean.
   *(Situada na costa, a cidade oferece vistas espetaculares do oceano.)*

10. **Faced with budget cuts**, the school had to reduce its staff.
    *(Diante de cortes orçamentários, a escola teve que reduzir seu quadro de funcionários.)*

---

grammar.title: 2.11 Linking Words and Discourse Markers Avançados
grammar.content:
No B2, o aluno deve usar conectores sofisticados para construir argumentos complexos e articular ideias com coesão.

#### Contraste e Concessão

| Conector | Uso | Exemplo |
|----------|-----|---------|
| although / even though | + clause | Although it was raining, we went out. |
| despite / in spite of | + noun / -ing | Despite the rain, we went out. |
| however | início de frase (formal) | The plan was good. However, it failed. |
| nevertheless / nonetheless | apesar de tudo | He was tired. Nevertheless, he kept working. |
| whereas / while | contraste direto | She is outgoing, whereas he is shy. |
| on the other hand | alternativa contrastante | It's expensive. On the other hand, it's excellent quality. |
| much as | concessão enfática | Much as I admire her, I disagree with her decision. |

**Exemplos:**

1. **Although** the project was challenging, the team delivered it on time.
   *(Embora o projeto fosse desafiador, a equipe o entregou no prazo.)*

2. **Despite having** very little experience, she handled the situation brilliantly.
   *(Apesar de ter muito pouca experiência, ela lidou com a situação brilhantemente.)*

3. The company reported strong profits. **However**, many employees were laid off.
   *(A empresa relatou lucros fortes. No entanto, muitos funcionários foram demitidos.)*

4. **Even though** he apologized several times, she refused to forgive him.
   *(Mesmo tendo se desculpado várias vezes, ela se recusou a perdoá-lo.)*

5. The product is innovative. **Nevertheless**, it has failed to attract consumers.
   *(O produto é inovador. Mesmo assim, não conseguiu atrair consumidores.)*

6. **Whereas** some people prefer working from home, others thrive in an office environment.
   *(Enquanto algumas pessoas preferem trabalhar de casa, outras prosperam em ambiente de escritório.)*

7. The restaurant is quite far. **On the other hand**, the food is outstanding.
   *(O restaurante é bastante longe. Por outro lado, a comida é excelente.)*

8. **In spite of** the doctor's warnings, he continued to smoke.
   *(Apesar dos alertas do médico, ele continuou a fumar.)*

9. **Much as** I appreciate your help, I need to do this on my own.
   *(Por mais que eu aprecie sua ajuda, preciso fazer isso sozinho.)*

10. **While** technology has brought many benefits, it has also created new problems.
    *(Embora a tecnologia tenha trazido muitos benefícios, ela também criou novos problemas.)*

#### Causa e Consequência

| Conector | Uso |
|----------|-----|
| due to / owing to | + noun (causa formal) |
| as a result / consequently / therefore | consequência |
| since / as / given that | causa (início de frase) |
| so... that / such... that | consequência enfática |
| thereby | consequência formal + -ing |

**Exemplos:**

1. The flight was canceled **due to** severe weather conditions.
   *(O voo foi cancelado devido a condições climáticas severas.)*

2. **Since** you have more experience, you should lead the project.
   *(Uma vez que você tem mais experiência, você deveria liderar o projeto.)*

3. He didn't prepare properly. **As a result**, he failed the interview.
   *(Ele não se preparou adequadamente. Como resultado, falhou na entrevista.)*

4. **Given that** the economy is slowing down, we need to cut costs.
   *(Dado que a economia está desacelerando, precisamos cortar custos.)*

5. The noise was **so** loud **that** I couldn't concentrate.
   *(O barulho era tão alto que eu não conseguia me concentrar.)*

6. It was **such** a complicated process **that** many people gave up.
   *(Era um processo tão complicado que muitas pessoas desistiram.)*

7. **Owing to** a technical issue, the website was temporarily unavailable.
   *(Devido a um problema técnico, o site ficou temporariamente indisponível.)*

8. She invested wisely and **consequently** became financially independent.
   *(Ela investiu sabiamente e consequentemente se tornou financeiramente independente.)*

9. **As** the deadline is approaching, we need to work faster.
   *(Como o prazo está se aproximando, precisamos trabalhar mais rápido.)*

10. The new policy reduced paperwork, **thereby** increasing efficiency.
    *(A nova política reduziu a burocracia, aumentando assim a eficiência.)*

#### Adição e Ênfase

| Conector | Uso |
|----------|-----|
| furthermore / moreover / what is more | adição formal |
| in addition to | + noun / -ing |
| not only... but also | ênfase dupla |
| besides | além disso |
| above all | acima de tudo |

**Exemplos:**

1. The hotel is conveniently located. **Furthermore**, it offers excellent service.
   *(O hotel tem uma localização conveniente. Além disso, oferece um serviço excelente.)*

2. **In addition to** her professional achievements, she is also a dedicated volunteer.
   *(Além de suas conquistas profissionais, ela também é uma voluntária dedicada.)*

3. **Not only** is she a talented musician, **but she is also** a gifted painter.
   *(Ela não é apenas uma musicista talentosa, mas também uma pintora dotada.)*

4. **Moreover**, the study found that sleep deprivation affects memory.
   *(Além disso, o estudo descobriu que a privação de sono afeta a memória.)*

5. **What is more**, the company plans to expand internationally.
   *(Mais do que isso, a empresa planeja expandir internacionalmente.)*

6. He is brilliant. **Besides**, he is incredibly hardworking.
   *(Ele é brilhante. Além do mais, é incrivelmente trabalhador.)*

7. We need to consider the costs. **Above all**, we need to think about the impact on employees.
   *(Precisamos considerar os custos. Acima de tudo, precisamos pensar no impacto sobre os funcionários.)*

8. **In addition to** speaking four languages, she also has a law degree.
   *(Além de falar quatro idiomas, ela também tem um diploma de direito.)*

9. The apartment is spacious. **What is more**, it has a stunning view of the city.
   *(O apartamento é espaçoso. Mais ainda, tem uma vista deslumbrante da cidade.)*

10. **Not only did** the company increase salaries, **but it also** introduced flexible working hours.
    *(A empresa não apenas aumentou os salários, como também introduziu horários flexíveis.)*

---

grammar.title: 2.12 Gerunds and Infinitives — Diferenças Sutis de Significado
grammar.content:
No B2, é crucial dominar os verbos que mudam de significado dependendo de serem seguidos por gerúndio ou infinitivo.

#### Remember / Forget

- **remember + gerund** = lembrar de algo que JÁ aconteceu
- **remember + infinitive** = lembrar de fazer algo (que ainda não foi feito)

**Exemplos:**

1. I **remember locking** the door. (Eu me lembro de ter trancado a porta.)
2. **Remember to lock** the door before you leave. (Lembre-se de trancar a porta antes de sair.)
3. I'll never **forget meeting** the president. (Nunca vou esquecer de ter conhecido o presidente.)
4. Don't **forget to meet** me at the airport. (Não esqueça de me encontrar no aeroporto.)
5. She **remembers being** very happy as a child. (Ela se lembra de ter sido muito feliz quando criança.)
6. He **forgot to bring** his wallet. (Ele esqueceu de trazer a carteira.)
7. I **remember hearing** that song for the first time. (Lembro-me de ter ouvido aquela música pela primeira vez.)
8. Did you **remember to send** the email? (Você se lembrou de enviar o e-mail?)
9. She'll never **forget visiting** the Grand Canyon. (Ela nunca vai esquecer de ter visitado o Grand Canyon.)
10. **Don't forget to turn** off the lights. (Não esqueça de apagar as luzes.)

#### Stop

- **stop + gerund** = parar de fazer algo (a atividade cessa)
- **stop + infinitive** = parar para fazer algo (interromper para fazer outra coisa)

**Exemplos:**

1. He **stopped smoking** two years ago. (Ele parou de fumar há dois anos.)
2. She **stopped to smoke** a cigarette on her way to work. (Ela parou para fumar um cigarro no caminho para o trabalho.)
3. The children **stopped playing** when it started raining. (As crianças pararam de brincar quando começou a chover.)
4. We **stopped to admire** the view from the mountain. (Paramos para admirar a vista da montanha.)
5. Can you **stop making** that noise? (Você pode parar de fazer esse barulho?)
6. He **stopped to think** about what he should say. (Ele parou para pensar no que deveria dizer.)
7. She **stopped eating** meat when she was 18. (Ela parou de comer carne quando tinha 18 anos.)
8. They **stopped to rest** at a small village along the way. (Eles pararam para descansar em um vilarejo no caminho.)
9. Please **stop talking** during the presentation. (Por favor, parem de conversar durante a apresentação.)
10. I **stopped to read** the sign on the wall. (Parei para ler a placa na parede.)

#### Try

- **try + gerund** = experimentar, testar algo como possível solução
- **try + infinitive** = fazer um esforço para conseguir algo

**Exemplos:**

1. **Try pressing** Ctrl+Alt+Delete. (Tente pressionar Ctrl+Alt+Delete.) [= experimente]
2. I **tried to open** the door, but it was stuck. (Tentei abrir a porta, mas estava emperrada.) [= fiz esforço]
3. If you can't sleep, **try drinking** warm milk. (Se não consegue dormir, tente tomar leite morno.)
4. She **tried to convince** him, but he wouldn't listen. (Ela tentou convencê-lo, mas ele não quis ouvir.)
5. **Try turning** it off and on again. (Tente desligar e ligar novamente.)
6. He **tried to reach** the top shelf, but he was too short. (Ele tentou alcançar a prateleira de cima, mas era baixo demais.)
7. Have you **tried adding** more salt? (Você já tentou adicionar mais sal?) [= experimentou?]
8. I'll **try to finish** the report before Friday. (Vou tentar terminar o relatório antes de sexta.)
9. **Try taking** a different route to avoid traffic. (Tente pegar um caminho diferente para evitar trânsito.)
10. They **tried to negotiate** a better deal, but the seller refused. (Eles tentaram negociar um acordo melhor, mas o vendedor recusou.)

#### Regret

- **regret + gerund** = arrepender-se de algo que fez
- **regret + infinitive** = lamentar ter que dizer/informar algo (formal)

**Exemplos:**

1. I **regret telling** her the truth. (Me arrependo de ter contado a verdade a ela.)
2. We **regret to inform** you that your application has been unsuccessful. (Lamentamos informar que sua candidatura não foi bem-sucedida.)
3. She **regrets not studying** harder at university. (Ela se arrepende de não ter estudado mais na universidade.)
4. I **regret to say** that the company will be closing. (Lamento dizer que a empresa vai fechar.)
5. He **regrets spending** so much money on that car. (Ele se arrepende de ter gasto tanto dinheiro naquele carro.)
6. We **regret to announce** the cancellation of tonight's performance. (Lamentamos anunciar o cancelamento da apresentação desta noite.)
7. Do you **regret leaving** your hometown? (Você se arrepende de ter deixado sua cidade natal?)
8. I **regret to inform** you that your flight has been canceled. (Lamento informá-lo de que seu voo foi cancelado.)
9. She deeply **regrets not accepting** the scholarship. (Ela se arrepende profundamente de não ter aceitado a bolsa.)
10. The university **regrets to advise** that the program will not run this year. (A universidade lamenta informar que o programa não será oferecido este ano.)

---

grammar.title: 2.13 Articles — Uso Avançado
grammar.content:
No B2, o uso correto de artigos em contextos mais sofisticados é fundamental.

#### Regras avançadas e exceções

**Sem artigo (zero article) — generalizações:**

1. **Life** is too short to worry about small things.
   *(A vida é curta demais para se preocupar com coisas pequenas.)*

2. **Education** is the key to **success**.
   *(Educação é a chave para o sucesso.)*

3. **Water** boils at 100 degrees Celsius.
   *(A água ferve a 100 graus Celsius.)*

4. **Dogs** are often considered man's best friend.
   *(Os cães são frequentemente considerados o melhor amigo do homem.)*

5. She studied **medicine** at **Oxford University**.
   *(Ela estudou medicina na Universidade de Oxford.)*

**Com "the" — uso específico:**

6. **The water** in this lake is contaminated.
   *(A água neste lago está contaminada.)*

7. **The education** she received at that school changed her life.
   *(A educação que ela recebeu naquela escola mudou sua vida.)*

8. **The dogs** next door bark all night.
   *(Os cachorros da casa ao lado latem a noite toda.)*

9. He plays **the piano** beautifully. (instrumentos musicais usam "the")
   *(Ele toca piano lindamente.)*

10. **The rich** should help **the poor**. (adjetivos substantivados usam "the")
    *(Os ricos deveriam ajudar os pobres.)*

**Regras especiais com nomes geográficos:**

- **Com "the":** the United States, the Netherlands, the Amazon, the Alps, the Pacific Ocean, the Sahara Desert
- **Sem "the":** Brazil, Mount Everest, Lake Titicaca, Central Park, Oxford Street

---

grammar.title: 2.14 Subjunctive (Subjuntivo em Inglês)
grammar.content:
O subjuntivo é raro no inglês moderno, mas ainda aparece em contextos formais, especialmente após verbos como **suggest, recommend, insist, demand, propose, request** e em expressões como **it is essential/important/vital that**.

**Estrutura:** Sujeito + base form (sem "s" na terceira pessoa, sem "to")

**Exemplos:**

1. The doctor **recommended that** she **take** a week off work.
   *(O médico recomendou que ela tirasse uma semana de folga.)*

2. I **suggest that** he **be** more careful in the future.
   *(Sugiro que ele seja mais cuidadoso no futuro.)*

3. **It is essential that** every student **submit** the assignment on time.
   *(É essencial que todo aluno entregue o trabalho no prazo.)*

4. The committee **insisted that** the proposal **be reviewed** before the vote.
   *(O comitê insistiu que a proposta fosse revisada antes da votação.)*

5. **It is vital that** the company **invest** in new technology.
   *(É vital que a empresa invista em nova tecnologia.)*

6. She **demanded that** he **apologize** immediately.
   *(Ela exigiu que ele pedisse desculpas imediatamente.)*

7. The lawyer **requested that** the witness **be present** at the hearing.
   *(O advogado solicitou que a testemunha estivesse presente na audiência.)*

8. **It is important that** everyone **be** aware of the safety procedures.
   *(É importante que todos estejam cientes dos procedimentos de segurança.)*

9. The board **proposed that** the CEO **resign** immediately.
   *(O conselho propôs que o CEO renunciasse imediatamente.)*

10. **It is crucial that** the patient **not eat** anything before the surgery.
    *(É crucial que o paciente não coma nada antes da cirurgia.)*

---

grammar.title: 2.15 Emphasis and Focus Structures
grammar.content:
#### Do / Does / Did para ênfase

Em frases afirmativas, "do/does/did" pode ser adicionado antes do verbo principal para dar ênfase.

**Exemplos:**

1. I **do** understand your concerns.
   *(Eu realmente entendo suas preocupações.)*

2. She **does** work very hard.
   *(Ela realmente trabalha muito.)*

3. I **did** tell you about the meeting!
   *(Eu realmente te falei sobre a reunião!)*

4. We **do** appreciate your support.
   *(Nós realmente apreciamos seu apoio.)*

5. He **does** have a valid point.
   *(Ele realmente tem um argumento válido.)*

6. I **did** lock the door. I'm absolutely certain.
   *(Eu realmente tranquei a porta. Tenho absoluta certeza.)*

7. They **do** make an effort, but the results are poor.
   *(Eles realmente se esforçam, mas os resultados são fracos.)*

8. She **did** apologize, but it was too late.
   *(Ela realmente pediu desculpas, mas foi tarde demais.)*

9. I **do** think we should reconsider our options.
   *(Eu realmente acho que deveríamos reconsiderar nossas opções.)*

10. The government **does** need to take action on climate change.
    *(O governo realmente precisa agir sobre a mudança climática.)*

---

grammar.title: 2.16 Discourse Markers e Hedging Language
grammar.content:
**Hedging** é o uso de linguagem que suaviza afirmações, tornando-as menos categóricas. É fundamental em contextos acadêmicos e profissionais.

**Exemplos de hedging:**

1. The results **seem to suggest** that the treatment is effective.
   *(Os resultados parecem sugerir que o tratamento é eficaz.)*

2. This **could potentially** lead to significant changes.
   *(Isso poderia potencialmente levar a mudanças significativas.)*

3. **It appears that** the economy is recovering.
   *(Parece que a economia está se recuperando.)*

4. The evidence **tends to indicate** a correlation between the two factors.
   *(As evidências tendem a indicar uma correlação entre os dois fatores.)*

5. **To some extent**, the criticism is justified.
   *(Até certo ponto, a crítica é justificada.)*

6. This is **arguably** the most important discovery of the decade.
   *(Este é sem dúvida o mais importante descobrimento da década.)*

7. The project **is likely to** face some challenges.
   *(É provável que o projeto enfrente alguns desafios.)*

8. **It is widely believed that** artificial intelligence will transform the job market.
   *(É amplamente aceito que a inteligência artificial transformará o mercado de trabalho.)*

9. The results **may not necessarily** apply to all populations.
   *(Os resultados podem não necessariamente se aplicar a todas as populações.)*

10. **As far as I can tell**, the system is working correctly.
    *(Pelo que posso perceber, o sistema está funcionando corretamente.)*

---

reading.title: Text 1: The Hidden Cost of Convenience
reading.content:
In the early hours of a Tuesday morning, a delivery truck pulls up to an apartment complex in central Manchester. The driver, whose shift started at 4 a.m., unloads dozens of small parcels — most of them containing items that could easily have been purchased at any shop within walking distance. A tube of toothpaste here, a phone charger there, a single book that could have been borrowed from the library. This scene, which plays out across thousands of cities every single day, is a quiet illustration of a much larger problem: the environmental and human cost of our addiction to convenience.

The rise of online shopping has transformed the way we live. What was once a weekly trip to the high street has been replaced by a series of taps on a screen. In the United Kingdom alone, online retail sales exceeded one hundred and twenty billion pounds in 2024, and the trend shows no sign of slowing down. Consumers have grown accustomed to next-day delivery, free returns, and an apparently limitless selection of products. For many, the idea of physically going to a shop feels almost archaic.

Yet this convenience comes at a price that is seldom reflected in the checkout total. The environmental footprint of online shopping is staggering. Each parcel generates packaging waste — cardboard boxes, plastic wrapping, polystyrene padding, and adhesive tape — much of which ends up in landfills. According to a report by the Environmental Investigation Agency, the United Kingdom produces approximately five million tonnes of packaging waste annually, a figure that has risen by twenty per cent in the last decade. Furthermore, the carbon emissions associated with the transportation of individual parcels are far greater than those generated by a single trip to a well-stocked local shop, where goods arrive in bulk.

The human cost is equally troubling. Delivery drivers, many of whom are classified as independent contractors rather than employees, often work grueling shifts with little job security, no paid holidays, and minimal benefits. Investigations by journalists and labor rights organizations have revealed that some drivers are under such pressure to meet delivery targets that they skip meals, avoid bathroom breaks, and drive for hours without rest. The gig economy, which underpins much of the delivery infrastructure, has been widely criticized for exploiting workers while generating enormous profits for a handful of corporations.

There are those who argue that online shopping is, in fact, more efficient than traditional retail. They point to studies suggesting that a single delivery van serving multiple households produces fewer emissions per item than individual car journeys to shops. While there is some truth to this argument, it fails to account for several critical factors: the surge in returns (which often result in products being destroyed rather than resold), the energy consumed by vast warehouse operations, and the psychological effect of frictionless purchasing — the tendency for consumers to buy more than they need simply because it is so easy to do so.

What, then, is the solution? It would be naive to suggest that online shopping should be abandoned entirely. For people with disabilities, those living in remote areas, or individuals with demanding work schedules, it offers a genuine lifeline. The answer lies not in prohibition but in mindfulness. Consumers can consolidate their orders to reduce the number of deliveries, choose slower shipping options (which are generally more carbon-efficient), support local businesses when possible, and resist the impulse to buy items they do not truly need. Retailers, for their part, must invest in sustainable packaging, fair labor practices, and transparent reporting of their environmental impact.

Convenience is not inherently harmful. But when it becomes the default mode of existence — when we are no longer willing to walk ten minutes to buy bread because a drone can deliver it to our doorstep in thirty — we must ask ourselves what kind of world we are building. The cost of convenience is not measured only in pounds and pence. It is measured in carbon, in waste, in the well-being of the workers who make it all possible, and ultimately, in the health of the planet we share.

#### Glossary

| Word/Expression | Meaning in Portuguese |
|----------------|----------------------|
| parcels | pacotes / encomendas |
| high street | rua principal (com lojas) |
| archaic | arcaico |
| staggering | impressionante / chocante |

| grueling | exaustivo / esgotante |
| gig economy | economia de trabalhos temporários/freelancers |
| frictionless | sem atrito / sem dificuldade |
| lifeline | tábua de salvação |
| mindfulness | atenção plena / consciência |

#### Comprehension Questions

**Q2.** What are the main environmental consequences of online shopping mentioned in the text?

**Q4.** The text mentions an argument in favor of online shopping's efficiency. What is it, and why does the author consider it incomplete?

**Q5.** What practical solutions does the author propose for both consumers and retailers?

reading.title: Text 2: The Psychology of Decision-Making
reading.content:
Every day, from the moment we open our eyes until we fall asleep, we make decisions. Some are trivial — what to have for breakfast, which route to take to work — while others carry profound consequences: whether to accept a job offer, end a relationship, or invest our savings. Psychologists estimate that the average adult makes approximately thirty-five thousand decisions per day, the vast majority of which are made unconsciously, guided by mental shortcuts that our brains have developed over millennia. Understanding how and why we make the decisions we do is one of the most fascinating — and practically useful — areas of modern psychology.

One of the most influential concepts in decision-making research is the idea of **cognitive biases**, systematic patterns of deviation from rational judgment. The term was popularized by the psychologists Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky, whose groundbreaking work in the 1970s and 1980s revealed that human beings are far less rational than economists had traditionally assumed. Among the dozens of biases they identified, several are particularly relevant to everyday life.

The **confirmation bias** is the tendency to seek out, interpret, and remember information that confirms our pre-existing beliefs while ignoring evidence that contradicts them. For example, a person who believes that a particular political party is corrupt will readily absorb news stories that support this view while dismissing positive reports as propaganda or exceptions. This bias is especially dangerous in the age of social media, where algorithms curate content based on users' previous interactions, creating echo chambers that reinforce existing opinions and make it increasingly difficult to encounter diverse perspectives.

The **sunk cost fallacy** is the tendency to continue investing time, money, or effort into something because of what has already been spent, rather than evaluating whether future investment is worthwhile. A classic example is the person who sits through a terrible film at the cinema because they have already paid for the ticket, rather than leaving and doing something more enjoyable. In business, this bias can lead to catastrophic decisions, such as pouring millions of dollars into a failing project because "we've already invested too much to walk away."

**Anchoring** is the tendency to rely too heavily on the first piece of information encountered when making a decision. When negotiating the price of a used car, for instance, the seller's initial asking price serves as an anchor. Even if the buyer knows the car is overpriced, they are likely to negotiate downward from that anchor rather than making an independent assessment of the car's value. Retailers exploit this bias constantly: a shirt originally priced at eighty pounds that is "on sale" for forty feels like a bargain, even if the shirt was never truly worth eighty pounds to begin with.

The **availability heuristic** is the tendency to judge the likelihood of events based on how easily examples come to mind. People who have recently watched a news report about a plane crash, for example, tend to overestimate the probability of dying in a plane crash, even though statistically, flying is far safer than driving. This heuristic explains why people often fear dramatic, highly publicized risks (terrorism, shark attacks) while ignoring far more common dangers (heart disease, traffic accidents).

Perhaps the most psychologically complex bias is **loss aversion**, the principle that losses feel roughly twice as painful as equivalent gains feel pleasurable. This means that losing fifty pounds hurts far more than finding fifty pounds feels good. Loss aversion influences everything from investment decisions (people hold onto losing stocks for too long, hoping they will recover) to everyday choices (people stick with their current phone provider even when a better deal is available, because switching feels like a risk).

Understanding these biases does not make us immune to them — even Kahneman himself admitted that knowing about biases did not prevent him from falling prey to them. However, awareness is the first step toward better decision-making. By recognizing when our judgment might be compromised, we can slow down, seek out contradicting evidence, consult others, and make more deliberate choices. In a world that demands thirty-five thousand decisions a day, even a marginal improvement in the quality of those decisions can have a transformative effect on our lives.

#### Glossary

| Word/Expression | Meaning in Portuguese |
|----------------|----------------------|
| trivial | trivial / sem importância |
| millennia | milênios |
| cognitive biases | vieses cognitivos |
| groundbreaking | revolucionário / inovador |
| echo chambers | câmaras de eco |
| sunk cost fallacy | falácia do custo irrecuperável |
| anchoring | ancoragem |
| heuristic | heurística (atalho mental) |
| loss aversion | aversão à perda |
| to fall prey to | cair vítima de |

#### Comprehension Questions

**Q1.** Approximately how many decisions does an adult make per day, and how are most of them made?

**Q2.** What is the confirmation bias, and why is it particularly dangerous in the age of social media?

**Q3.** Explain the sunk cost fallacy using an example from the text.

**Q4.** How do retailers exploit the anchoring bias?

**Q5.** According to the text, what is the relationship between knowing about biases and being affected by them?

*(Respostas no Gabarito Geral — Seção 7)*

reading.title: Text 3: Migration, Identity, and Belonging
reading.content:
When Amara's family left Lagos, Nigeria, she was eleven years old. She remembers the airport — the noise, the queues, her mother clutching a folder of documents with both hands as though it contained the family's entire future, which in many ways it did. The flight to London was the first time Amara had ever been on an airplane, and she spent most of it pressed against the window, watching the earth shrink beneath her until the clouds swallowed it whole. She did not know then that the journey she was beginning would last far longer than the eight hours it took to cross the Sahara and the Mediterranean.

Amara's story is not exceptional. According to the International Organization for Migration, there are approximately two hundred and eighty-one million international migrants worldwide, a figure that represents roughly three and a half per cent of the global population. People leave their countries for countless reasons: war, persecution, economic hardship, climate change, the desire for better educational opportunities, or simply the hope that life might be different — perhaps even better — somewhere else. The decision to migrate is rarely taken lightly. It involves leaving behind not only a physical place but an entire network of relationships, traditions, and cultural references that constitute one's identity.

For Amara, the first years in London were marked by a persistent sense of dislocation. She spoke English fluently — Nigeria's colonial history had ensured that — but her accent drew attention. Classmates would ask her to repeat words, not because they hadn't understood, but because the way she pronounced them was interesting, exotic, different. She learned quickly that in England, the way you spoke mattered almost as much as what you said. She began, gradually and without fully realizing it, to modify her speech, softening the sharp vowels and clipped consonants of Nigerian English until she sounded, at least to casual ears, indistinguishable from her London-born classmates.

This process of linguistic and cultural adaptation is something that virtually all migrants experience to varying degrees. Sociologists refer to it as **acculturation** — the gradual adoption of the cultural norms, values, and behaviors of the host society. Acculturation is not a binary process; it does not require the migrant to abandon their heritage entirely in order to belong. Instead, most migrants navigate a complex, often contradictory space between the culture they brought with them and the culture they encounter. They may speak one language at home and another at school. They may celebrate certain holidays from their country of origin while also participating in the traditions of their new home. They may eat their mother's cooking on weekday evenings and fish and chips on Friday nights. This duality, though enriching, can also be exhausting.

The question of belonging is perhaps the most profound challenge that migrants face. Amara, now in her late twenties and working as a journalist in London, describes herself as both Nigerian and British, yet she confesses that she sometimes feels fully neither. When she visits Lagos — which she does every other year — relatives comment on how "English" she has become. When she is in London, colleagues occasionally ask where she is "really" from, a question that, however well-intentioned, implies that her Britishness is somehow conditional or incomplete. This experience of being caught between two worlds, of belonging everywhere and nowhere simultaneously, is so common among migrants and their descendants that psychologists have given it a name: **bicultural identity conflict**.

Research suggests that bicultural identity conflict can have significant effects on mental health, particularly when individuals feel pressure to choose one identity over another. However, studies also show that people who successfully integrate multiple cultural identities tend to be more creative, more empathetic, and more cognitively flexible than those who identify with a single culture. The ability to see the world through multiple lenses — to understand that there is more than one valid way to live, think, and communicate — is not a weakness. It is, in many respects, a superpower.

Amara has come to embrace this complexity. She writes about migration, identity, and belonging, not as abstract concepts but as lived realities. In a recent column, she wrote: "Home is not a place. It is a feeling, and feelings are portable. I carry Lagos in the way I laugh, in the pepper soup my mother taught me to make, in the Yoruba proverbs that surface in my mind when English fails me. And I carry London in the confidence I've gained, in the career I've built, in the friendships that have shaped who I am. I am not half of one thing and half of another. I am, quite simply, all of it."

The world is more interconnected than it has ever been, and migration — whether voluntary or forced — will continue to shape societies, economies, and cultures for generations to come. How we respond to this reality — whether with fear and suspicion or with openness and curiosity — will define not only the experience of migrants like Amara but the character of the communities that receive them. The story of migration is, ultimately, the story of humanity itself: a species perpetually in motion, seeking safety, opportunity, and the ever-elusive sensation of belonging.

#### Glossary

| Word/Expression | Meaning in Portuguese |
|----------------|----------------------|
| clutching | agarrando / segurando firmemente |
| dislocation | deslocamento / sensação de não pertencer |
| acculturation | aculturação |
| heritage | herança cultural |
| duality | dualidade |
| well-intentioned | bem-intencionado |
| bicultural identity conflict | conflito de identidade bicultural |
| to embrace | abraçar / aceitar |
| portable | portátil / transportável |
| ever-elusive | sempre elusivo / sempre difícil de alcançar |

#### Comprehension Questions

**Q1.** What details does the text use to convey the significance of Amara's family's departure from Lagos?

**Q2.** According to the text, what is acculturation, and why is it described as "not a binary process"?

**Q3.** What is "bicultural identity conflict," and what situations in Amara's life illustrate this concept?

**Q4.** The text mentions both negative and positive effects of having multiple cultural identities. What are they?

**Q5.** In Amara's own words (from her column), how does she define "home"? What does this reveal about her relationship with identity?

*(Respostas no Gabarito Geral — Seção 7)*
